The University of Tennessee at Chattanooga

**The University of Tennessee at Chattanooga** (**UT Chattanooga**, **UTC**, or **Chattanooga**)[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a [public university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") in [Chattanooga, Tennessee](/wiki/Chattanooga,_Tennessee "Chattanooga, Tennessee"), United States. It was founded in 1886 and is part of the [University of Tennessee System](/wiki/University_of_Tennessee_System "University of Tennessee System").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Tennessee_at_Chattanooga&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/UTCSign.jpg/250px-UTCSign.jpg)](/wiki/File:UTCSign.jpg)

UTC was founded in 1886 as the then-private and racially exclusive **Chattanooga University**, which was soon merged in 1889 with the [Athens](/wiki/Athens,_Tennessee "Athens, Tennessee")-based Grant Memorial University (now [Tennessee Wesleyan University](/wiki/Tennessee_Wesleyan_University "Tennessee Wesleyan University")),[[6]](#cite_note-6) becoming the Chattanooga campus of **[U.S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") Memorial University**.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 1907, the school changed its name to **University of Chattanooga**. In 1964 the university merged with [Zion College](/wiki/Zion_College "Zion College"), which had been established in 1949 and later became Chattanooga City College. In 1969 the University of Chattanooga joined the UT system and became the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The University of Chattanooga Foundation Inc. is a private corporation, created in 1969, that manages the university's private endowment.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Tennessee_at_Chattanooga&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Administration")]

UTC uses the [semester](/wiki/Semester "Semester") system, with five optional "mini-terms" in the summer. The leadership of the campus rests upon the [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_(education) "Chancellor (education)"), who answers to the [UT System President](/wiki/University_of_Tennessee_System "University of Tennessee System"). The university is currently headed by Chancellor Lori Mann Bruce.

### Leadership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Tennessee_at_Chattanooga&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Leadership")]

The following people have had the post of president or chancellor at the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga (UTC). Prior to 1969, the institution was known as the University of Chattanooga (1907–1969), U.S. Grant University (1889–1907), and Chattanooga University (1886–1889). At the time of UTC's establishment in 1969, the title of the leader became chancellor instead of president.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Tennessee_at_Chattanooga&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Academics")]

Chattanooga is best known for its nationally ranked [Business](/wiki/Business "Business") program,[[14]](#cite_note-16) and its [Engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"), [Nursing](/wiki/Nursing "Nursing"), [English](/wiki/English_studies "English studies"), [Chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), [Accounting](/wiki/Accounting "Accounting"), [Psychology](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology"), [Music](/wiki/Music "Music"), and [Education](/wiki/Pedagogy "Pedagogy") departments. The university offers over 140 undergraduate majors and concentrations, and over 50 undergraduate minors.[[15]](#cite_note-utc.edu-17) Chattanooga also offers nearly 100 graduate programs and concentrations,[[15]](#cite_note-utc.edu-17) including a highly ranked master's program in [Industrial and Organizational Psychology](/wiki/Industrial_and_Organizational_Psychology "Industrial and Organizational Psychology"),[[16]](#cite_note-18) and [PhD](/wiki/PhD "PhD") programs in Computational Engineering and [Physical Therapy](/wiki/Physical_Therapy "Physical Therapy"). In an effort to expand the horizons of its student body, UTC began an exchange program with [Kangnung National University](/wiki/Kangnung_National_University "Kangnung National University") of [Kangnung, South Korea](/wiki/Kangnung "Kangnung").

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Tennessee_at_Chattanooga&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Research")]

SimCenter is UTC's computational engineering and simulation center. In November 2005, SimCenter was listed as the 89th most powerful supercomputer by [Top500](/wiki/Top500 "Top500").[[22]](#cite_note-24) On November 20, 2007, the university announced that the center had been named a National Center for Computational Engineering.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] More recently, the SimCenter provided the academic research for a new source of alternative energy unveiled by Bloom Energy Corporation in Sunnyvale, California.[[23]](#cite_note-25)

The Clinical Infectious Disease Control Research Unit is a research interest group composed of UTC faculty, students, and local partners.[[24]](#cite_note-hea-26) Members of the CIDC have had their research published in peer-reviewed journals, as well as presented at professional meetings and conferences.[[24]](#cite_note-hea-26)

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Tennessee_at_Chattanooga&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Campus")]

The university is served by [CARTA](/wiki/Chattanooga_Area_Regional_Transportation_Authority "Chattanooga Area Regional Transportation Authority") bus routes 4, 7, 10, 14, 19, and 28. Route 14 only operates on weekdays during fall and spring terms, when the university is session. The route runs on and off the campus on McCallie, Houston, Vine, Douglas, Fifth, and Palmetto Streets. A recent extension serves Third, O'Neal, and Central Streets, as well as Erlanger Hospital, and a large parking lot at [Engel Stadium](/wiki/Engel_Stadium "Engel Stadium"). All students showing valid university identification cards (MocsCards) ride for free on all CARTA routes, year-round.

Patten Chapel is one of the busiest sanctuaries in Chattanooga. Mostly weddings and memorial services are held there. A bride's room has been prepared and is always ready. Reserving the chapel should be done around a year in advance as its popularity sees events almost every weekend. Wedding receptions are not hosted at the chapel.

### Lupton Hall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Tennessee_at_Chattanooga&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Lupton Hall")]

Formerly the Lupton Memorial Library, Lupton Hall, named for [T. Cartter and Margaret Rawlings Lupton](/wiki/Cartter_Lupton "Cartter Lupton"), was constructed in 1974 to replace the aging John Storrs Fletcher Library (which has since been restored and renamed Fletcher Hall). As of 2005, the library's collection includes nearly 2 million items, including the [Fellowship of Southern Writers](/wiki/Fellowship_of_Southern_Writers "Fellowship of Southern Writers") archives. In 2008, the university was granted funding to build a new library.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

The university broke ground in 2010 for the new $48 million 180,000-square-foot (17,000 m2) library. Construction was completed on the UTC Library in January 2015.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

Following the opening of the new UTC Library, Lupton Hall began renovations in 2018.[[27]](#cite_note-29) Opening to students and faculty in 2020, Lupton's entire 116,000 square feet of available space had been gutted and rebuilt to include student centers, academic departments under the College of Arts and Sciences, various classroom spaces, and a new on-campus restaurant, Freshens.[[28]](#cite_note-30)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Tennessee_at_Chattanooga&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Chattanooga Mocs](/wiki/Chattanooga_Mocs "Chattanooga Mocs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Chattanooga_Mocs_logo.svg/250px-Chattanooga_Mocs_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Chattanooga_Mocs_logo.svg)

Athletics logo

Chattanooga's colors are [navy](/wiki/Navy_blue "Navy blue") and [old gold](/wiki/Old_gold "Old gold"). Their men's teams and athletes are nicknamed Mocs, and women's teams and athletes are Lady Mocs. Chattanooga athletics teams compete in [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") ([FCS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision") for [football](/wiki/College_football "College football")) in the [Southern Conference](/wiki/Southern_Conference "Southern Conference") (SoCon) and have been ranked as a national top 100 athletic program by The [National Association of Collegiate Directors of Athletics](/wiki/National_Association_of_Collegiate_Directors_of_Athletics "National Association of Collegiate Directors of Athletics") (NACDA) in the Division I Learfield Sports Director's Cup.[[29]](#cite_note-31)

Basketball

Main articles: [Chattanooga Mocs men's basketball](/wiki/Chattanooga_Mocs_men%27s_basketball "Chattanooga Mocs men's basketball") and [Chattanooga Mocs women's basketball](/wiki/Chattanooga_Mocs_women%27s_basketball "Chattanooga Mocs women's basketball")

Chattanooga's men's basketball program has been among the best in the Southern Conference since joining the league in 1977–78. The Mocs have won 10 SoCon Tournament titles, tied for first all-time with former member West Virginia and Davidson, 10 regular-season league championships prior to the change to the division format in 1995 and seven division titles, for 27 total titles. In [1997](/wiki/1997_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Tournament "1997 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Tournament"), led by coach [Mack McCarthy](/wiki/Mack_McCarthy "Mack McCarthy") and Chattanooga native Johnny Taylor, the Mocs made a run to the Sweet 16 as a No. 14 seed, beating Georgia and Illinois before falling to Providence. Before making the move to Division I, Chattanooga won the Division II National Championship in 1977.[[30]](#cite_note-32) In July 2008, the team was ranked number 48 on the [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") list of the most prestigious basketball programs since the 1984–85 season.[[31]](#cite_note-33)

The Mocs won the SoCon tournament [once again in 2009](/wiki/2009_Southern_Conference_men%27s_basketball_tournament "2009 Southern Conference men's basketball tournament"). Defeating the [College of Charleston Cougars](/wiki/College_of_Charleston_Cougars_men%27s_basketball "College of Charleston Cougars men's basketball") 80–69 in the championship game on their home court at the [McKenzie Arena](/wiki/McKenzie_Arena "McKenzie Arena"), the Mocs punched their ticket to the [NCAA tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Tournament "NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Tournament"), their first since [2005](/wiki/2005_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Tournament "2005 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Tournament").

[Jimmy Fallon](/wiki/Jimmy_Fallon "Jimmy Fallon") from *[Late Night with Jimmy Fallon](/wiki/Late_Night_with_Jimmy_Fallon "Late Night with Jimmy Fallon")* chose the Mocs as his team of choice going into the [2009 NCAA tournament](/wiki/2009_NCAA_Men%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Tournament "2009 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Tournament"). The Wednesday night (March 18) show included a live Skype chat with Head Coach John Shulman, as well as representatives of the pep band and cheerleading squads made in studio. Fallon's house band [The Roots](/wiki/The_Roots "The Roots") wrote and performed an ode to Shulman titled, "The Don Juan of the SoCon" and Shulman and his six seniors (Nicchaeus Doaks, Zach Ferrell, Kevin Goffney, Khalil Hartwell, Stephen McDowell and Keyron Sheard) made an in-studio appearance following their tournament game with UConn.

The Lady Mocs are the most successful women's basketball program in Southern Conference history, with 15 regular season titles since 1983–1984, 10 consecutive conference championships at the end of 2008–2009, and 14 overall conference championships.[[32]](#cite_note-gomocs.com-34)

Golf

The men's golf squad won its third consecutive Southern Conference trophy and finished 18th in the NCAA Championships in 2009.

In August 2012, UTC golfer [Steven Fox](/wiki/Steven_Fox_(golfer) "Steven Fox (golfer)") won the U.S. Amateur Championship.

Women's golf posted a 3.46 team GOA in the spring while advancing to the NCAA Division I finals in just the second year of the program since disbanding in the mid-1980s.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

Softball

The Mocs’ softball team has won 11 regular season titles and 10 SoCon Tournament Championships. They have also made 7 NCAA tournament appearances.[[32]](#cite_note-gomocs.com-34)

Wrestling

Main article: [Chattanooga Mocs wrestling](/wiki/Chattanooga_Mocs_wrestling "Chattanooga Mocs wrestling")

Chattanooga is home to the only NCAA Division I [wrestling](/wiki/Collegiate_wrestling "Collegiate wrestling") program in the state of Tennessee. The Mocs' wrestling team has won 8 of the past 9 SoCon titles since the 2012–2013 academic year.[[32]](#cite_note-gomocs.com-34)

Football

Main article: [Chattanooga Mocs football](/wiki/Chattanooga_Mocs_football "Chattanooga Mocs football")

The team plays in the Southern Conference (SoCon) in Division I FCS (formerly I-AA). [Hall of Famer](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") [Terrell Owens](/wiki/Terrell_Owens "Terrell Owens") played wide receiver for the Mocs from 1992 to 1995. The team won three straight SoCon championships from 2013 to 2015. They play in [Finley Stadium](/wiki/Finley_Stadium "Finley Stadium"), which hosted the [NCAA Division I Football Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship "NCAA Division I Football Championship") from 1997 to 2009.

In 2021, the team fired its offensive line coach, Chris Malone, for making a derogatory social media post about Georgia and civil rights leader [Stacey Abrams](/wiki/Stacey_Abrams "Stacey Abrams").[[34]](#cite_note-36)

## University nickname

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Tennessee_at_Chattanooga&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: University nickname")]

The school's athletic teams are called the Mocs. The teams were nicknamed the Moccasins until 1996. (The origin of the name is uncertain; however, [Moccasin Bend](/wiki/Moccasin_Bend "Moccasin Bend") is a large horseshoe-shaped bend in the [Tennessee River](/wiki/Tennessee_River "Tennessee River") directly below [Lookout Mountain](/wiki/Lookout_Mountain "Lookout Mountain").)

The mascot has taken on four distinct forms. A [water moccasin](/wiki/Water_moccasin "Water moccasin") was the mascot in the 1920s, and then a [moccasin](/wiki/Moccasin_(footwear) "Moccasin (footwear)") shoe (known as "The Shoe") was used as the school's mascot at times in the 1960s and 1970s. From the 1970s until 1996, the mascot was [Chief Moccanooga](/wiki/Chief_Moccanooga "Chief Moccanooga"), an exaggerated [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee "Cherokee") tribesman.

In 1996, the Moccasins name and image were dropped in favor of the shortened "Mocs" and an [anthropomorphized](/wiki/Anthropomorphism "Anthropomorphism") [northern mockingbird](/wiki/Northern_mockingbird "Northern mockingbird"), in accordance with the state bird, named "[Scrappy](/wiki/Scrappy_Moc "Scrappy Moc")" dressed as a [railroad engineer](/wiki/Railroad_engineer "Railroad engineer"). The school's main athletic logo features Scrappy riding a train (a reference to Chattanooga's history as a major [railroad](/wiki/Railroad "Railroad") hub and to the song "[Chattanooga Choo Choo](/wiki/Chattanooga_Choo_Choo "Chattanooga Choo Choo")"). The mascot takes its name from former [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") coach [A. C. "Scrappy" Moore](/wiki/Andy_Moore_(American_football) "Andy Moore (American football)").
